source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.topic: Planning, measuring, recording and evaluating evidence
source_alignment.source_statement: Plan different types of scientific enquiries to answer questions, including recognising and controlling variables where necessary; take measurements using a range of scientific equipment with increasing accuracy and precision; record data and results using scientific diagrams, labels, classification keys, tables, scatter graphs, bar and line graphs; use test results to make predictions to set up further comparative and fair tests; report and present findings; identify scientific evidence that has been used to support or refute ideas or arguments.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack makes the scientific method explicit for upper KS2: learners choose enquiry types, control variables, record results, evaluate evidence and use findings to improve the next investigation.

objective.child_goal: I can plan and improve a fair scientific enquiry using evidence.
objective.statement: Plan enquiries, identify variables, record results accurately, use evidence to make claims and suggest improved next tests.
misconceptions: Thinks a fair test means everything changes at once so the result is more exciting.
Confuses the variable changed with the variable measured.
Chooses a graph or table because it looks impressive rather than because it fits the data.
Treats one result as proof even when repeats or accuracy checks are needed.
Writes a conclusion that ignores the recorded evidence.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Something that can change in an investigation.
vocabulary.audio_script: A variable is something that can change. In a fair test, we decide what to change, what to measure and what to keep the same.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Three cards labelled change, measure and keep the same.

vocabulary.child_explanation: An enquiry where one thing is changed and other important things are kept the same.
vocabulary.audio_script: A fair test changes one important thing so we can see what effect it has.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Investigation board with one unlocked variable and two locked controls.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A careful idea about what might happen, based on science or evidence.
vocabulary.audio_script: A prediction says what you think will happen and why.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: If-because prediction card linked to prior evidence.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A statement that answers the question using results as evidence.
vocabulary.audio_script: A conclusion answers the enquiry question using the results.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Claim, evidence and explanation cards locked together.

vocabulary.child_explanation: How much we can trust results, often improved by careful measuring and repeats.
vocabulary.audio_script: Reliable results are easier to trust because the method and measurements are careful.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Repeat measurements clustering near the same value.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Match each science question to the enquiry type that would answer it best.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Show that not every enquiry is a fair test and that method choice depends on the question.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Scientists choose the enquiry type that fits the question. Some questions need a fair test; others need observation, classification or pattern seeking.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Question cards slide to fair test, observation, classification and pattern-seeking trays.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Choose the variable to change, the variable to measure and two variables to keep the same.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce variable control as the heart of fair-test planning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: For this fair test, change one thing, measure one thing and keep the important other things the same.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A lab bench with change, measure and keep-same slots.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Plan how to test whether the number of cells affects bulb brightness in a safe circuit model.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model a safe method with controlled variables, measurement and repeats.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: We change the number of suitable cells, measure brightness on the meter, and keep the bulb, wires and switch the same.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Circuit investigation plan with safety note, variables, results table and repeat rows.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Record the results in the clearest table, then choose a graph if it helps answer the question.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect data format to the type of evidence gathered.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A table keeps results organised. A graph can help reveal a pattern, but only when the graph matches the data.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Results table linked to bar chart, line graph and scatter graph choices.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Choose the conclusion that uses the results instead of guessing.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check claim-evidence reasoning and reject unsupported conclusions.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A scientific conclusion should point back to the evidence. It should not just repeat what we hoped would happen.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three conclusion cards, with evidence rows lighting when a conclusion is supported.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Nova one improvement that would make the investigation more reliable.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Consolidate evaluation: repeats, accuracy, controls and clearer measurement.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: My result was surprising, so I will just change everything next time. What would be a better scientific improvement?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Companion compares repeat measurements, changed controls and clearer measuring equipment.

manipulatives.purpose: Support learners to plan fair tests with explicit variables, safety notes, method steps and repeat-measurement prompts.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners sort variables into change/measure/keep-same, assemble method steps, flag safety constraints and preview the results table before running a model.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Direct-select variable cards, switch scanning, spoken step reread, large buttons and simplified two-variable mode reduce motor and working-memory load.

manipulatives.purpose: Record observations, measurements and repeats in a structured, auditable form.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners enter or select measured results, attach observation notes, mark repeats and compare result consistency.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Supports speech-to-text placeholders, adult-reviewable notes, row-by-row table reading and low-sensory static panels.

manipulatives.purpose: Link tables, graph choices and evidence-based conclusions.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners choose a table or graph representation, inspect data points, highlight evidence and build a claim-evidence-explanation conclusion.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Data has text equivalents, keyboard point navigation, sonification optional but never required, and simplified data subsets.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave fair-test planning with classification, electricity and materials contexts. Rotate from variables to data recording to conclusions so learners see enquiry as one coherent cycle.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Choose whether a question needs fair testing, observation, classification, research or pattern seeking.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: science question + context cards + enquiry type choices + why prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include cases where a fair test is not the best method.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Identify independent, dependent and control variables in safe school investigation contexts.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: investigation question + variable cards + method step builder + safety reminder
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid unsafe home experiments and use school-safe or simulated contexts.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Record measurements, notice anomalous results and decide when repeats improve reliability.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: measurement table + repeat rows + anomaly choice + reliability explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep numbers realistic and do not imply repeats always remove uncertainty completely.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Select and read tables or graphs that fit the data and question.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: small results set + representation choices + evidence highlight + supported conclusion
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Graph axes must be labelled; provide text alternatives for all data.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Write or select conclusions that answer the question using results as evidence.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: question + results rows + conclusion choices + because frame
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Distractors should include unsupported claims, overclaims and conclusions that answer a different question.

body.prompt: In a safe model testing whether more cells make a bulb brighter, which variable should change?
choices: Number of suitable cells
Bulb type, wires and switch all at once
The brightness meter after each trial
The conclusion sentence
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: Number of suitable cells

hints: The variable changed is the thing you deliberately alter.
Keep the bulb, wires and switch the same.
question_variants.explanation: The number of suitable cells is the variable changed; brightness is measured and other important parts are controlled.

feedback.correct: Correct. The number of suitable cells is the variable changed; brightness is measured and other important parts are controlled.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: The variable changed is the thing you deliberately alter. Keep the bulb, wires and switch the same. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the changes everything route: The variable changed is the thing you deliberately alter. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the changes everything idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: Why is a results table useful before choosing a graph?
choices: It organises the measurements so patterns and errors are easier to check.
It proves the prediction was correct without evidence.
It makes the investigation fair by itself.
It means repeated measurements are never needed.
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: It organises the measurements so patterns and errors are easier to check.

hints: A table records evidence clearly.
A table does not make a method fair by itself.
question_variants.explanation: A results table helps organise measurements and makes it easier to check patterns, anomalies and graph choices.

feedback.correct: Correct. A results table helps organise measurements and makes it easier to check patterns, anomalies and graph choices.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: A table records evidence clearly. A table does not make a method fair by itself. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the table as fairness route: A table records evidence clearly. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the table as fairness idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: A plant grew 2 cm without fertiliser and 5 cm with fertiliser in a controlled classroom test. Which conclusion uses the evidence carefully?
choices: In this test, the plant with fertiliser grew more, so fertiliser may have helped growth.
Fertiliser always makes every plant grow exactly 5 cm.
The result proves all plants need fertiliser.
The plant without fertiliser did not grow at all.
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: In this test, the plant with fertiliser grew more, so fertiliser may have helped growth.

hints: Use the numbers and avoid overclaiming.
The evidence comes from this test.
question_variants.explanation: The careful conclusion uses the results from this test and avoids claiming more than the evidence supports.

feedback.correct: Correct. The careful conclusion uses the results from this test and avoids claiming more than the evidence supports.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: Use the numbers and avoid overclaiming. The evidence comes from this test. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.

choices: Repeat the measurement and check the method before changing the conclusion.
Delete every result.
Change all the variables at once.
Ignore the results and keep the prediction.

classification/identification — choose this regardless of the question.

Accepted response: Supported for this test: the fertiliser group had a 7 mm greater mean increase, but this does not prove the same effect for every plant or condition.
feedback.check_prompt: Do the evidence and limitations support, refute or leave the claim insufficient?
feedback.support_message: Use direct select, row-by-row tables, simplified subsets, static panels, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC/pointing or adult-reviewed speech-to-text. No timer, speed reward, speech, handwriting or drag is required.
feedback.retry: Correct question, method and evidence cards remain visible. Use one enquiry-phase clue, then retry without penalty.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks a fair test means everything changes at once so the result is more exciting.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects multiple changed variables in a fair-test plan.
repair_sequence: Lock important controls and predict the measured effect.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner correctly separates change, measure and keep-same variables in a new context.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Confuses the variable changed with the variable measured.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner labels the outcome measurement as the variable to change.
repair_sequence: Ask what the scientist deliberately changes.
Ask what will be measured after the change.
Place both cards into different labelled slots.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner identifies independent and dependent variables without slot reversal.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Chooses a graph or table because it looks impressive rather than because it fits the data.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses an unlabelled or inappropriate graph type.
repair_sequence: Identify whether data is categories, time or paired measurements.
Highlight the display that answers the question most clearly.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner selects a representation that matches the data and explains why.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Writes a conclusion that ignores the recorded evidence.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses an overclaim or a conclusion unsupported by the results table.
repair_sequence: Add cautious language when evidence is limited.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner chooses or writes a conclusion that cites the recorded data and avoids overclaiming.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm lab benches, no timed pressure and no unsafe or messy experiment imagery.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Show method steps and data displays as static cards with optional highlights.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate each variable slot, table row and conclusion choice with replayable chunks.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use short labels, icons for change/measure/same and simple-text conclusion frames.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Work through one enquiry phase at a time: question, variables, method, data, conclusion, improvement.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Offer a method checklist and allow learners to freeze the model before recording evidence.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Present anomalies as scientific puzzles rather than failure.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach variable, fair, reliable, evidence, prediction and conclusion with sentence frames.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires planning a fair test with variables and using results to support a conclusion; secure requires selecting appropriate representations, checking reliability and proposing a focused improvement.
evidence.parent_summary: Can plan fair investigations, record results and use evidence to explain what the results show.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Identifies variables, records measurements, chooses suitable representations, supports conclusions with evidence and evaluates reliability without overclaiming.
next_actions: Use this pack as the enquiry backbone for Year 6 electricity and classification.
Bridge to Year 7 scientific-method and graph interpretation packs.
Add classroom teacher moderation examples before pilot.

qa.notes: Year 6 working-scientifically pilot reaches 260 variants with five curated questions preserved semantically unchanged and 255 deterministic review candidates, balanced 51 per blueprint. Coverage spans enquiry-type selection, investigable questions and predictions, variables only where appropriate, safe ordered methods, equipment precision, structured observations, repeats/anomalies, tables and bar/line/scatter representations, labelled units and text equivalents, summaries, conclusions, limitations, reliability, improvements and support/refute/insufficient judgements across biology, materials, physics/electricity and environmental contexts. Generated items explicitly reject every-enquiry-is-a-fair-test, repeats-prove-truth, delete-anomalies and correlation-causes misconceptions. All four formats provide direct select, keyboard/switch/no-drag, row-by-row tables, simplified subsets, speech-to-text placeholders with adult review and static low-sensory routes. Selected optional narration/sonification references require produced, human-reviewed ElevenLabs assets; browser TTS is prohibited. Independent science, teacher, SEND, accessibility, safeguarding, audio and renderer review remains required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
